perturbation.original_text: The Parties acknowledge that Anaheim is a public entity subject to the California Public Records Act, and the Ralph M. Brown Act; except to the extent permitted by law, the Parties may, without violating this Agreement, disclose matters that are made confidential by this Agreement to governmental officials or the public as required by any law, regulation, order, rule, ruling or other requirement of law (“Law”).
perturbation.changed_text: The Parties acknowledge that Anaheim is a public entity subject to the California Public Records Act, and the Ralph M. Brown Act; except to the extent permitted by law, the Parties may, without violating this Agreement, disclose matters that are made confidential by this Agreement to governmental officials or the public as required by any law, regulation, order, rule, ruling or other requirement of law (“Law”). Nothing contained in this Agreement or in any discussions undertaken, or disclosures made pursuant hereto shall: (a ) be interpreted or relied upon, directly or indirectly, by either Part as a commitment or intent to engage in a business relationship, contract , or futur e dealings with the other Party; and (b ) prevent either Party from entering into similar discussions with unrelated third parties so long as such discussions do not violate the terms and conditions imposed by this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: Moving the statement 'Nothing contained in this Agreement...' into the section about public record disclosures obscures its meaning and enforceability, as it's not logically related to the California Public Records Act or the Ralph M. Brown Act. This creates confusion as to whether this provision modifies the public disclosure allowance.
perturbation.law_explanation: The modification obscures the explicit exceptions allowed under the CPRA.  The added text regarding 'no binding commitment' creates potential ambiguity and could be misconstrued as limiting Anaheim's obligation to disclose information under the CPRA, which contradicts the CPRA's mandate for transparency.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 404 | Cal Cities Oh no! Sorry about that. Can’t seem to find that page. Let’s see if we can help you get to where you want to be. Home Advocacy Education and Events Get Involved News Resources About Us
perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces a structural flaw by adding a 'no commitment' clause into the section about public record disclosures, which could confuse its applicability to CPRA requirements. The Law Explanation suggests that this addition could be misconstrued as limiting Anaheim's obligation to disclose information under the CPRA, which the LLM states contradicts the CPRA's mandate for transparency. Scraped Law Snippet 1 shows access to the California Code, GOV 6253, presumably the CPRA, and while the full text is not available, it is reasonable to infer that the altered text, by creating ambiguity, undermines the CPRA's purpose. This aligns with the Structural Flaws Legal Contradiction definition, where a required legal disclosure is placed in an irrelevant or misleading section, making it difficult to interpret.

perturbation.original_text: Recipient acknowledge s and agree s that in the event of any breach of this Agreement, the Disclosing Party would be irreparably and immediately harmed and could not be made whole by monetary damages. It is accordingly agreed that Disclosing Party, in addition to any other remed y to which it may be entitled in law or equity, shall be entitled to injunctive relief to prevent breaches of this Agreement, and to compel specific performance of this Agreement, without the need for proof of actual damages.
perturbation.changed_text: 11. Waiver . In no event shall either P arty be liable to the other for consequential, special, indirect, incidental, punitive or exemplary loss, damage , or expense relating to this Agreement. Recipient acknowledge s and agree s that in the event of any breach of this Agreement, the Disclosing Party would be irreparably and immediately harmed and could not be made whole by monetary damages. It is accordingly agreed that Disclosing Party, in addition to any other remed y to which it may be entitled in law or equity, shall be entitled to injunctive relief to prevent breaches of this Agreement, and to compel specific performance of this Agreement, without the need for proof of actual damages.
perturbation.explanation: Placing the waiver provision (paragraph 11) *before* the remedies section (paragraph 9 after modification) creates confusion. It could be interpreted that the waiver of consequential damages applies *even to* the injunctive relief remedy, undermining the Disclosing Party's ability to effectively enforce the agreement, because this could suggest that it can't collect on damages, even when seeking an injunction.
perturbation.law_explanation: The modification creates an internal inconsistency regarding remedies. While injunctive relief is generally available under California law (Cal. Civ. Code § 3366), the placement of the waiver *before* the remedies section could be interpreted as restricting the court's ability to grant full and adequate relief, if consequential damages are waived.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CIV 3366 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> Code Search >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Civil Code - CIV DIVISION 4. GENERAL PROVISIONS [3274 - 9566] ( Heading of Division 4 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 16. ) PART 1. RELIEF [3274 - 3428] ( Part 1 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 3. SPECIFIC AND PREVENTIVE RELIEF [3366 - 3424] ( Title 3 enacted 1872. ) CHAPTER 1. General Principles [3366 - 3369] ( Chapter 1 enacted 1872. ) 3366. Specific or preventive relief may be given as provided by the laws of this state. (Amended by Stats. 1905, Ch. 465.)
perturbation.justification: The placement of the waiver provision before the remedies section creates a structural flaw that leads to a legal contradiction. According to the Perturbation Explanation, this arrangement introduces confusion regarding whether the waiver of consequential damages applies even to injunctive relief, thus undermining the Disclosing Party's ability to enforce the agreement. The Law Explanation confirms this concern by stating that the placement could be interpreted as restricting the court's ability to grant full and adequate relief. While Scraped Law Snippet 1 (Cal. Civ. Code § 3366) indicates that specific or preventive relief may be given as provided by the laws of this state, the altered contract structure potentially limits the scope of this relief by seemingly waiving consequential damages. This creates a contradiction where the availability of a legally sanctioned remedy is undermined by the contract's structure. The Perturbation Type, 'Structural Flaws Legal Contradiction,' directly applies as the organization of the contract affects its enforceability by potentially misleading parties about the extent of available remedies.
